texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Global climate change leads to high interest in technologies for CO2 capture and storage (CCS) (Gupta et al., 2003; McKee, 2002; Steeneveldt et al., 2006). Among these, post combustion CO2 capture (PCC) by reactive absorption with aqueous amine solutions is one of the most promising technologies. The process has been used for many years in different industrial applications like natural gas treatment (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997) so that it could be implemented quickly for PCC. Furthermore, it could be used for retrofits of existing power plants (Ewert, 2005; Göttlicher, 2003). texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reactive absorption is one of the most promising technologies for post combustion CO2 capture (PCC). There is a need for methods and equipment that allow studying PCC efficiently on scales that are many orders of magnitude smaller than those of future installations in power plants. The present work describes in detail the set-up and operation of a pilot plant that is suited for that purpose. In that pilot plant the closed cycle of the absorption/desorption process is continuously operated (column diameters: 0.125 m, absorber/desorber packing height: 4.2/2.5 m, packing type: Sulzer Mellapak 250.Y TM , flue gas flow rate: 30-110 kg/h, CO2 partial pressure: 35-135 mbar, solvent flow rate: 50-350 kg/h). The plant is equipped with high-end measuring instrumentation and is operated using an automated process control system. Sampling points allow the determination of the liquid phase concentration profiles in both columns. Two reference experiments that were carried out with an aqueous solution of 0.3 g/g monoethanolamine (MEA) are discussed in detail. The results give insight in the process behaviour in general and in the way such plants should be operated and how the evaluation of the results should be carried out. Based on comprehensive experimental studies with aqueous MEA solutions the influence of all important process parameters like CO2 partial pressure, CO2 removal rate, fluid dynamic load of the absorber, solvent flow rate, desorber pressure and solvent composition on the process behaviour is discussed in detail. These results allow a comprehensive understanding of the absorption/desorption process, which is important not only for the development of new tailor-made solvents but also for the design and operation of large scale plants. Furthermore the data establish a base line for comparisons with new solvents tested in the pilot plant and can be used for a validation of models of the PCC process with MEA.

texts.orig: for post combustion CO2 capture are orders of magnitude smaller than those that would be installed in power plants. Much of the equipment, like absorbers and desorbers, that would be needed for PCC in a full size power plant is larger than any of its kind that has been built before. Even more important is the fact that the energy requirement of the absorption/desorption process presently still is exceedingly high. Reliable numbers are very difficult to obtain, but for the most widely discussed solvent, an aqueous solution of 0.3 g/g monoethanolamine (which will be called 'MEA' for simplicity in the following), the number for the specific regeneration energy seems to converge to values of about 3.6-3.8 GJ/tCO 2 (Knudsen et al., 2009; Mimura et al., 1997) which would lead to a drop of the thermal efficiency of the power plant in the range of approximately 11-15 percent points including CO2 compression (Page et al., 2009; Göttlicher, 2003). Approximately 70-80% of the total energy demand of the absorption/desorption process including the CO2 compression is needed as thermal energy for the solvent regeneration (Aaron and Tsouris, 2005). texts.orig: of the plant itself, e.g. by the use of an interstage cooler in the absorber (Chang and Shih, 2005), and the use of better solvents (Notz et al., 2007). Latter one is the aim of a large number of research projects carried out world-wide (CASTOR, 2004; CESAR, 2008; BIGCO2, 2007; CCP, 2000; CO2CRC, 2003). In most of these studies MEA is chosen as a reference PCC solvent to which new solvents are compared. For evaluating new solvents and testing process modifications pilot plants are needed. If these are suitably designed and operated the results from the pilot plant operation will allow a model based scale-up to the power plant scale. Such a plant is described in the present paper. It was set up in the frame of the EU project CASTOR (CASTOR, 2004) and has successfully been operated since 2006 for testing solvents in that project as well as in the follow-up EU project CESAR (CESAR, 2008) and in co-operations with industry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are fewer reports on experimental pilot plant studies with the solvent MEA (e.g. Chapel et al., 1999; Knudsen et al., 2009, 2011; Mangalapally et al., 2009; Mimura et al., 1995). Various pilot plant data are published by Tobiesen et al. (2007, 2008) in the context of validation of a rigorous absorber and desorber model respectively. Since their results are published separately for absorber and desorber, the influence of process parameters on the coupled absorption/desorption process for CO2 capture from flue gas is not discussed. Dugas (2006) presents numerous pilot plant data with the focus on the mass transfer performance of the absorber and desorber respectively, but not on the influence of process parameters on the regeneration energy requirement of the coupled absorption/desorption process for CO2 capture from flue gas. None of these publications cover systematic pilot plant studies, in which the influence of all important process parameters on the CO2 capture performance is discussed in comparable details as the present study.

None of these publications cover systematic pilot plant studies, in which the influence of all important process parameters on the CO2 capture performance is discussed in comparable details as the present study.

texts.orig: The goal of the present paper is to present the plant itself, critically discuss its operation and the data evaluation as well as to describe extensive pilot plant studies with MEA. As most of this is not plant specific, the main conclusions should also hold for other plants of this type, many of which are presently being built or were put in operation recently (Ewers and Moser, 2009; Schreier et al., 2009; Jockenhövel et al., 2009).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the development of new solvents a comprehensive understanding about the influence of process parameters on the process behaviour, especially on the regeneration energy requirement, is important. For that reason systematic parameter studies with MEA have been carried out in that pilot plant and are discussed in the present work. In total eight different variation studies are carried out, for which one single parameter is varied. In the first four variation studies, the flue gas conditions are kept constant and only directly controlled process parameters of the absorption/desorption plant are changed. These parameter studies cover the variation of the solvent flow rate, the desorber pressure, the solvent composition and the solvent temperature. In the last four variation studies operation parameters are kept constant and boundary conditions are varied. These parameter studies cover the variation of the CO2 partial pressure, the fluid dynamic load, the CO2 removal rate as well as the flue gas temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flue gas at the absorber inlet is provided by a commercial gas-fired burner designed for heating homes. The burner can be operated in two modes and produces flue gas either with approx. 3.8 vol% CO2 or with approx. 6 vol% CO2 (both numbers refer to the dry gas). In the first case the flue gas is similar to that of a combined cycle gas turbine power plant (Rolker and Arlt, 2006). To study flue gas conditions similar to coal fired power plants with their higher CO2 content (Rolker and Arlt, 2006), captured CO2 from the desorber top can be recycled. There is also an option for adding CO2 or traces of NO x and SO2 from gas bottles to the flue gas. The experiments presented in this paper were carried out without adding NO x and SO2.
texts.text: The flue gas at the absorber inlet is provided by a commercial gas-fired burner designed for heating homes. The burner can be operated in two modes and produces flue gas either with approx. 3.8 vol% CO2 or with approx. 6 vol% CO2 (both numbers refer to the dry gas). In the first case the flue gas is similar to that of a combined cycle gas turbine power plant (Rolker and Arlt, 2006). To study flue gas conditions similar to coal fired power plants with their higher CO2 content (Rolker and Arlt, 2006), captured CO2 from the desorber top can be recycled. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flue gas from the burner first passes a blower. Before entering the pre washer and the absorber the flue gas flow is split as the amount of flue gas produced by the burner cannot be freely specified. Only a part enters the pre washer, the rest is directly fed to the chimney. The flue gas temperature after the blower is approx. 70-80 ◦ C. For setting a typical flue gas temperature at the absorber inlet of 45-50 ◦ C a pre washer, which is realized as direct contact cooler, is used. The heat is removed in a plate heat exchanger, which is installed in the washing water loop, cooled by cooling water. For the whole pilot plant cooling water of approx. 5-10 ◦ C is available. The condensate is removed discontinuously through valve V10. The flue gas mass flow at the absorber inlet can be controlled in three ways: by the valve in the bypass flow into the chimney, by the frequency of the blower, and by the throttle valve V12 in the main flue gas line. The flue gas flow rate through the absorber is limited due to fluid dynamic reasons. At a solvent flow rate of 300 kg/h, the maximum F-factor is approx. 2.4 Pa 0.5 corresponding to a flue gas flow rate of approx. 110 kg/h. The amount and condition of the flue gas at the absorber inlet are determined in the following way: measurement of volume flow (F1, vortex flow meter), pressure (P1) and temperature (T5), online gas analysis of CO2 and O2 concentration in the dry gas (Q2, CO2 by IR absorption, O2 by paramagnetism), determination of the water partial pressure assuming that the flue gas leaves the pre washer saturated at the outlet temperature, calculation of the N2 concentration from the concentration summation equation neglecting trace components. From a measurement it is known that the NO x concentrations are approx. 13 vol ppm NO and 3 vol ppm NO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The absorber has five packing sections, each of which contains a liquid redistributor at the top and a collector at the bottom. Each collector contains a sampling line and a Pt 100 thermometer so that the temperature and concentration profiles can be determined. The liquid distributor at the bottom of the collector is realized as horizontal tubing, which contains a horizontal slot over the whole length. Each packing section is equipped with four elements of the structured packing Sulzer Mellapak 250.Y TM , the overall packing height in each section is 0.84 m. The flue gas is fed into the absorber below the lowest of the five packing sections, the lean solvent above the highest. For reducing amine losses a washing section is installed at the top of the absorber above the lean solvent feed. This sixth packing section is identical with the five others except of the packing height of 0.42 m. Before the treated gas leaves the absorber column above the washing section, it has to pass a demister to retain liquid droplets. For monitoring the fluid dynamic behaviour in the absorber, the differential pressure over the whole column is measured (P4).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both mass flow rate and temperature of the lean solvent entering the absorber are controlled. The mass flow rate is measured by a Coriolis measurement device (F3). The required flow rate is reached by automatic control of the pump frequency (pump P3). The temperature of the lean solvent (T6) is controlled by the opening of valve V90 for cooling water through the heat exchanger W3. The mass flow rate and the density of the rich solvent leaving the absorber are measured by a Coriolis measurement device (F19). To ensure steady state operation, the rich solvent is withdrawn level (L2) controlled by pump P2. There are sampling points both for the lean and the rich solvent (valves V84 and V80).
texts.text: Both mass flow rate and temperature of the lean solvent entering the absorber are controlled. The mass flow rate is measured by a Coriolis measurement device (F3). The required flow rate is reached by automatic control of the pump frequency (pump P3). The temperature of the lean solvent (T6) is controlled by the opening of valve V90 for cooling water through the heat exchanger W3. The mass flow rate and the density of the rich solvent leaving the absorber are measured by a Coriolis measurement device (F19). To ensure steady state operation, the rich solvent is withdrawn level (L2) controlled by pump P2. There are sampling points both for the lean and the rich solvent (valves V84 and V80).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the washing section, the water is recycled from the liquid collector to the top of the section by pump P7 (flow measurement F17) and cooled in the plate heat exchanger W5. To avoid accumulation of amine in the washing water, deionised water is continuously fed (F8). The liquid collector at the bottom of the washing section has an overflow through which excess washing water is removed, which is led into the absorber. The condition of the flue gas at the outlet of the washing section is measured in the same way as at the absorber inlet (F4, P8, T22, Q4). Again, it is assumed that the gas is saturated with water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rich solvent leaving the absorber is pumped through the heat exchanger W4 (so called rich-lean heat exchanger) in which it is heated up by the hot lean solvent from the desorber bottom. To avoid evaporation of CO2 in the heat exchanger and in the feed line to the desorber, the throttle valve V57 is installed at the desorber inlet and set so that the pressure P9 is sufficiently high. After passing the throttle valve the solvent enters a flash vessel, which is located directly above the desorber packing inside the desorber column. From there the liquid solvent is directly distributed on the top of the desorber packing. The released gas leaves the flash vessel at the top outlet, where it is combined with the vapor flow from the desorber packing sections. The desorber is built up of three packing sections which are constructed in the same way as in the absorber. The stripping steam is generated in the desorber bottom by partial evaporation of the liquid solvent with electrical heating elements, for which the electrical power is measured. The hot lean solvent at the desorber bottom flows through the rich-lean heat exchanger W4 and through the heat exchanger W3 before entering the absorber top. The composition of the lean solvent can be determined by analysis of a liquid sample from valve V84.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The condensate from W2 is collected in the tank B1, which is equipped with a level control by pump P5 to ensure steady state operation. The condensate is either recycled to the washing section or removed by valve V53 as waste water (measurement of the volume in a certain time), to fulfill the water balance of the whole process. This is important for steady state operation of the plant. Due to the level control in absorber and in the condensate tank B1 the desorber bottom level will change, if the total water balance is not fulfilled. The desorber bottom level is thus controlled by valve V53.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The water balance of the whole plant is determined by two feed streams (flue gas, fresh water feed at the absorber washing section) and three outlet streams (lean flue gas, CO2 product stream, waste water leaving through valve V53). The water mass flow rates in the three gaseous streams are determined by their temperatures due to water saturation. The water loss through the CO2 product stream gas is low (usually a temperature less than 20 ◦ C is reached in this plant). Since a certain amount of fresh water make-up is required for the absorber washing section, this means for minimization of the waste water flow, that the flue gas temperature at the outlet of the absorber washing section should be slightly above the flue gas temperature at the absorber inlet. On the other hand, the temperature at the outlet of the absorber washing section also has an important influence on the amine recovery in the washing section. For the design of the absorption/desorption process it is important to take into account the tight coupling of these two effects.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pilot plant is controlled by a programmable controller (SPC, Twin-CAT1 Version v2.9.0, Beckhoff Industrie Elektronik), which runs on an industrial PC installed in the control cabinet of the plant. The user interface for plant operation is realized in the software LabVIEW (Version 7.1, National Instruments). The operator has access to time plots of all relevant process variables as well as derived quantities like the CO2 removal rate /TAB or the specific evaporator energy q evap . This allows assessing if steady state operation of the plant is reached. During plant operation all plant data are stored each 10 s. All these information are available for the evaluation and characterization of operating points as explained below. The accuracy of the main measurement devices is given in the following. The temperature measurements have an accuracy of 0.1 K based on calibration of the whole measurement chain. The precision of the pressure measurements is 0.075% from the upper limit of the measurement range, which means an accuracy of approx. 1-2 mbar for the different measurement positions. The accuracy of the main flow measurement devices is 0.2-1% of the measured value. More details about the measurement devices and the process control system are given by Notz (2010).

texts.orig: If the pilot plant is already filled with solvent, it takes approximately 3 h to start-up the plant and to reach a steady state. Only after a steady state operation at the desired state point of at least 30 min is maintained liquid samples are taken, which takes another 15 min. To avoid influence of the sampling on the measured column profiles, the liquid samples are taken from the column bottom to the column top.

To avoid influence of the sampling on the measured column profiles, the liquid samples are taken from the column bottom to the column top.

In that case adjustable parameters like the evaporator heat are varied so that the desired set value is reached. This may require additional time. Typically one or two operating points are studied per day. The subsequent analysis of the liquid samples and the data evaluation typically takes one to two days per operating point.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The evaluation of the results of a steady state operating point with specified experimental conditions is done using a standardized spread sheet. The focus is on the absorption/desorption process at given flue gas conditions, the pre washer is not considered. Averaged values of all measured process variables from the process control system and the results of the liquid analysis are imported. The spread sheet creates temperature profiles, concentration profiles, a stream table, an overview table and a process flow diagram with all important data. As example, the latter one is shown in Figs. 3 and 4 for the two operating points 1 and 2 that will be discussed in more detail below. Furthermore, additional information that is calculated from the input data is provided, like column profiles for the partial pressure of CO2 and H2O and mass transfer profiles from gas to liquid for CO2 and H2O or operating lines in Mc Cabe Thiele type diagrams. The results are very helpful for a better understanding of the process behaviour in the absorber and desorber. The procedure used for calculating these additional properties is explained in Appendix A. Also information on energy and material balances is supplied and used for estimating heat losses as explained below. Physical properties like heat capacities, densities, water vapor pressure, heat of evaporation of water and heat of absorption, which are needed for the evaluation of experiments, are taken from Chiu and Li (1999), Kim and Svendsen (2007), Rowley et al. (2007) and Weiland et al. (1998).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Foaming is known as operational problem in plants for reactive absorption with amine solutions (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). It was also observed in some experiments carried out in the pilot plant described here. In these cases foaming leads to a strong fluctuation of the liquid level in the desorber bottom accompanied by a fluctuation of the temperature T17 measured in the liquid collector below the bottom packing in the desorber. Note that no significant increase of the pressure drop in the columns was observed when foaming occurred. As consequence of the fluctuation of the desorber bottom liquid level, it is no longer possible to adjust the waste water removal to fulfill the total water balance. To avoid these problems, an anti foaming agent was added in cases when the behaviour described above occurred. After adding an emulsion of 1 g of anti foaming agent (Merck, silicon based anti foaming agent 00107743.000100) in water to the approx. 70 kg of solvent in the pilot plant, the operational problems due to foaming always disappeared. No other consequences of the addition of the anti foaming agent were observed.

texts.orig: more detailed description is given in Appendix B. The quality of the analysis of the component mass fractions can be checked by the summation condition. For the MEA experiments in the pilot plant, deviations are below 2% in most cases. The deviations are not systematic, in particular no losses due to degradation were observed in this way. The GC chromatograms did not show a systematic increase of side components. In several samples a side product with less than 0.1 area % was observed, which could be neglected for further evaluations. To make sure that for further calculations the summation condition is fulfilled for samples of rich and lean solvent, for which all overall components were analyzed, the analytically determined concentrations are normalized.

texts.orig: The mass flow of the captured CO2 can be determined in different ways: on the one hand, it can be calculated as difference of the CO2 mass flows at the inlet and the outlet of the absorber. On the other hand, the mass flow of the CO2 product stream is directly measured. As both values are available online during plant operation they can be used for assessing, whether a steady state is reached. Furthermore, after the analysis of the liquid samples, the captured CO2 mass flow can also be calculated from the CO2 mass fractions in lean and rich solvent and the corresponding mass flows. A comparison of the captured CO2 mass flow in the absorber determined with the three different methods is shown in Fig. 5 for all MEA experiments that were carried out in the pilot plant. For most of the experiments, the deviation is less than 5% or even better. Furthermore, the component mass balances for N2, O2, CO2 and H2O in the absorber were checked. Deviations are below 2% for most experiments.

texts.orig: In this section results from two reference experiments, one at low CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas (experiment 1) and another one at high CO2 partial pressure (experiment 2) will be discussed in detail. In Table 5 the main process conditions of the two reference experiments are given. In the top section of the Table 5, all process variables are summarized, which have to be specified for plant operation, in the bottom section the main results from the two experiments are given. The full experimental data sets are given in Figs. 3 and 4. Experiment 1 with a CO2 partial pressure of 54.6 mbar in the flue gas corresponds to conditions in gas turbine power plants, whereas experiment 2 with a CO2 partial pressure of 109.4 mbar is closer to conditions in a coal fired power plant (Rolker and Arlt, 2006). The CO2 removal rate /TAB of the two experiments (75.9% for experiment 1 and 51.9% for experiment 2) are below the typical values of 90%. The reason for this is the comparatively low packing height especially in the absorber. Higher CO2 removal rates can be achieved but only at the expense of very high regeneration energies (cf. texts.orig: In contrary to the small height pilot plants a CO2 removal rate of 90% from the flue gas of the coal-fired power plant can be reached with reasonable regeneration energy requirement (minimum value of 3.6 GJ/tCO 2 for MEA) at the full height pilot plant at Esbjerg, whose absorber has been equipped with 17 m of the random packing IMTP50 (Knudsen et al., 2009). Other literature data for the specific energy requirement of MEA can be found from Tobiesen et al. (2008) and Dugas (2006). Because of a wide varity of operating conditions (e.g. solvent flow rate, CO2 partial pressure, CO2 removal rate, desorber pressure) a one-to-one comparison of single operating points of these data with our plant is not possible. Furthermore Tobiesen et al. (2007) and Tobiesen et al. (2008) do not present combined results for absorber and desorber. For rough comparison of these pilot plant data the range for the CO2 lean loading and the specific regeneration energy requirement can be evaluated (the relationship between these two variables is discussed in Section 4.2). For the pilot plant presented in that paper, the lean loading is in the range of 0.096-0.36 molCO2 / molMEA and the specific energy requirement is in the range of 3.7-10.2 GJ/tCO 2 for experiments with a desorber pressure of 2 bar and a solvent composition with 0.3 g/g MEA. The experimental data in Tobiesen et al. (2008) show a specific energy requirement of 3.7-11.2 GJ/tCO 2 for a lean loading of 0.22-0.41 molCO2 / molMEA at a desorber pressure of 2 bar. Dugas (2006) reports a specific energy requirement in the range of 5.1-14.2 GJ/tCO 2 for experiments with a lean loading of 0.14-0.37 molCO2 / molMEA at a desorber pressure of 1.6 bar. The higher values compared to Tobiesen et al. (2008) and our plant can be explained by the different desorber pressure and the fact, that Dugas (2006) is not using a rich-lean heat exchanger but a solvent preheater, which is undersized.

texts.orig: Experiment 1 can be interpreted so as it represents the upper part of a large scale absorber whereas experiment 2 represents the bottom part. This is illustrated in Fig. 6a which shows a plot of the CO2 partial pressure versus the packing height of the absorber. Fig. 6b shows the CO2 mass transfer profiles in the absorber. For the experiment 1 the highest CO2 mass flow rate is observed in the bottom part of the absorber whereas for experiment 2 the CO2 mass flow rate continuously increases from absorber bottom to the top. It can also be seen from Fig. 6b that as expected the overall amount of absorbed CO2 is higher for experiment 2 compared to experiment 1.

texts.orig: Fig. 7a shows an increase of the driving force for the CO2 mass transfer from the absorber bottom (high p CO2 ) to the absorber top (low p CO2 ) for experiment 2. It is closely related to the CO2 mass transfer profile from Fig. 6b which shows an increase of the amount of CO2 absorbed per segment from the bottom to the top of the absorber. For experiment 1 the driving force is almost constant. Only at the top of the absorber, there is a strong increase of the driving force due to the contact with the cold regenerated solvent. Despite this, the CO2 mass transfer profile in Fig. 6b shows a decrease of the CO2 mass transfer in the top section. The CO2 mass transfer is strongly influenced by the reaction kinetics of bicarbonate and carbamate formation in the liquid phase. A decreasing CO2 concentration in the liquid phase and a decreasing temperature both lead to a drop in the reaction kinetics, as can be seen at kinetic models for example in Blauwhoff et al. (1984). Thus low reaction kinetics in the upper part of the absorber due to low CO2 concentration and low temperature in experiment 1 is probably the reason for the low CO2 mass transfer.

texts.orig: The equilibrium curves for the experiments 1 and 2 as presented in Fig. 7a both show a minimum value for the CO2 loading in the upper part of the absorber. This is at a first glance astonishing but simply results from the fact that the absorber is not isothermal and the equilibrium values are calculated stage-wise with the corresponding temperature. The temperature maximum in the absorber, cf. Fig. 7b, is typical for the CO2 capture process, and is caused by the release of absorption enthalpy upon the absorption of CO2. The location and the shape of the temperature maximum depend on the L / G ratio and on the amount of the absorbed CO2.

texts.orig: The temperature profile is closely related to the partial pressure profile of water in the gas phase (compare Figs. 7b and 8a). The mass transfer profile of water from gas to liquid as presented in Fig. 8b shows that in the lower part of the absorber the gas stream takes up water whereas in the upper part of the absorber water vapor from the gas stream condensates. This behaviour is a consequence of the rising temperature from the absorber bottom upwards until the temperature maximum is reached and the following decrease of the temperature towards the absorber top. This temperature profile mainly influences the equilibrium partial pressure of water in the gas phase and thus leads to the water mass transfer as seen in Fig. 8b.

As consequence of the higher CO2 mass transfer the temperature below the washing section is higher for experiment 2. Because of that, the gas stream must be cooled down more in the washing section in experiment 2 compared to experiment 1. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 removal rate is directly determined by the conditions in the absorber: it is fixed if the absorber feed streams (gas and liquid) as well as the column dimensions are given. Therefore, if for a given flue gas and solvent circulation rate a specified CO2 removal rate shall be reached, the concentration of CO2 in the lean solvent is fixed. The same scenario can be discussed equivalently in the following way: from the specifications follows that the mass flow of absorbed CO2 is fixed. As a consequence also the concentration difference between rich and lean solvent is fixed. But transferring a given mass flow of CO2 from the gas to the liquid phase in the absorber requires also a certain driving force for the CO2 mass transfer which leads to the fact that not only the difference between the CO2 loading in lean and rich solvent is fixed but also the absolute values of the CO2 loadings. As consequence, the evaporator power in the desorber bottom must be set so that the required lean loading is reached in the regenerated solvent based on a given rich loading at the desorber inlet.

The same scenario can be discussed equivalently in the following way: from the specifications follows that the mass flow of absorbed CO2 is fixed. As a consequence also the concentration difference between rich and lean solvent is fixed. But transferring a given mass flow of CO2 from the gas to the liquid phase in the absorber requires also a certain driving force for the CO2 mass transfer which leads to the fact that not only the difference between the CO2 loading in lean and rich solvent is fixed but also the absolute values of the CO2 loadings. As consequence, the evaporator power in the desorber bottom must be set so that the required lean loading is reached in the regenerated solvent based on a given rich loading at the desorber inlet.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As Fig. 7a shows, the required lean loading is lower in experiment 1 (1.26 molCO2 /kg S ∗ ) compared to experiment 2 (1.53 molCO2 /kg S ∗ ). Thus the separation task in the desorber is more difficult in experiment 1. For that reason, the specific energy requirement of the evaporator after subtracting relevant heat losses (see Section 4.3) is higher in experiment 1 (5.01 GJ/tCO 2 ) compared to experiment 2 (3.98 GJ/tCO 2 ). This behaviour can be explained with the help of Figs. 12a and 13, which both are discussed below in detail. The lower the lean loading is, the lower is the corresponding equilibrium CO2 partial pressure at the desorber bottom for constant temperature and the slope of the equilibrium curve is less steep (see Fig. 12a). The latter one directly influences the slope of the operating line, which reflects the L / G ratio in the desorber and the stripping steam requirement respectively. Thus a decrease in the CO2 lean loading leads to an increase in the stripping steam requirement. Analysing the four contributions to the regeneration energy for the experiments 1 and 2 in Section 4.3, the results shown in Fig. 13 confirms the drastic increase of the energy requirement to provide stripping steam from experiment 1 to 2. This relationship between the CO2 lean loading and the stripping steam requirement is also confirmed by the parameter studies with an aqueous solution of 0.3 g/g MEA shown in the Sections 5.1, 5.5 and 5.6. For these parameter variations, the desorber pressure (and thus the desorber temperature) and the solvent composition are kept constant, so that the equilibrium curve at desorber temperature is similar for all these experiments. The evaluation of the experimental results shows again, that for these conditions the stripping steam requirement increases by decreasing lean loading.

texts.orig: In addition to the absorber profiles of the CO2 mass fraction in the liquid phase, Fig. 9 also shows the corresponding profiles in the desorber for both experiments 1 and 2. The shape of the CO2 mass fraction profiles in the desorber is very similar for both experiments. For experiment 2, there is a significant difference between the CO2 mass fraction of the rich solvent leaving the absorber and that entering the desorber. This is due to flashing of CO2 that occurred before the sampling position at the bottom of the flash vessel at the liquid inlet of the desorber. The flashing of CO2 can take place after the throttle valve V57 and/or in the rich-lean heat exchanger W4 during heating. This is also discussed in Section 4.3. Also the liquid flow at the desorber flash vessel is diluted by water coming from the washing section. This is usually only a minor effect on the CO2 mass fraction in the liquid sample from the flash vessel compared to flashing of CO2.

texts.orig: The mass transfer rates of CO2 and water in the desorber in both experiments are shown in Fig. 10. Negative numbers indicate a flow from liquid to gas. As expected, the CO2 desorption rate is highest in the desorber bottom (cf. Fig. 10a). It drops off to almost zero in the upper part of the desorber, showing that the desorber height is more than sufficient for the studied operating points. The effect of flashing of CO2 from the rich solvent line into the flash vessel at the desorber top or even already in the rich-lean heat exchanger W4 (see also discussion in Section 4.3) in experiment 2 can also be seen in the mass transfer profile (data point at z = 2.52 m).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the desorber bottom a water vapor stream is produced (cf. Fig. 10b). The water vapor reduces the CO2 partial pressure and thus acts as stripping stream. Furthermore this water vapor stream provides the enthalpy for desorbing CO2 as well as for heating up the solvent and condensate reflux. All these result in the increase of the CO2 partial pressure and the decrease of the water partial pressure going from the bottom of the desorber to its top (cf. Fig. 11). Note that for experiment 1 with its more difficult separation task in the desorber, the water partial pressure must be kept at a higher level than in experiment 2. This corresponds to more stripping steam and a lower CO2 partial pressure.

texts.orig: The temperature profile in the desorber is directly coupled to the CO2 concentration (or partial pressure) profile (cf. Fig. 12b). The temperature in the desorber bottom is higher in experiment 1 than in experiment 2 although both were carried out with the same evaporator power and at identical desorber pressure. This is a direct result from the required lower concentration of CO2 in the lean solvent in experiment 1. In the temperature profile of experiment 2 in Fig. 12b a temperature step between the solvent inlet and the bottom of the flash vessel can be recognized. This is an effect of mixing with the cooler wash water and especially of flashing of the rich solvent. The condensate reflux enters the top of the desorber washing section approximately with ambient temperature which explains the low gas temperatures measured at the top of the washing section (see Fig. 12b).

texts.orig: To evaluate the heat flows at the pilot plant, the energy balance is calculated for different control rooms: on the one hand the single heat exchangers W2, W3, W4 and W5 as well as pipline sections and the absorber and the desorber each including the corresponding washing section are considered. On the other hand the energy balance is also calculated for the whole absorption/desorption process including washing sections. The equations for the energy balance are always derived from the first law of thermodynamics (all energy balances are given in detail in Notz, 2010). Table 6 gives an overview of the heat flows in the different heat exchangers in the pilot plant for the experiments 1 and 2. Heat is supplied to the process in the desorber bottom heat exchanger which is electrically heated. The set point of the supplied electrical power and the measured values differ by up to 400 W, only the measured value is used for the evaluation. The heat exchanger W1 in the washing water cycle is not considered here in detail as it is only used to set the flue gas inlet temperature which is also measured. The comparison of the heat duty in the condenser W2 at the desorber top shows a higher number for experiment 1 than for experiment 2. This can be explained by the higher stripping steam requirement already discussed in the previous section. As the solvent flow rate and temperatures are similar in both experiments, the heat duties of the rich-lean solvent heat exchanger W4 and the heat exchanger W3 for cooling the solvent before entering the absorber differ only by less than 10 % in both experiments. In the pilot plant, the temperature difference between the hot and cold side in the rich-lean heat exchanger is about 5-10 K. texts.orig: Considering the whole absorption/desorption process as black box, it can be concluded that in stationary operation, to a first approximation the sum over all heat duties should be zero, i.e. the sum of the supplied and removed heat fluxes should have equal magnitudes. This is a consequence of the fact that all components which are gaseous upon entering the plant leave the plant also as gases and the same holds for the liquids (i.e. water), and that the temperatures of the streams entering and leaving the plant do not strongly differ. A comparison with the experimental numbers given in Table 6 shows that for both experiments, there is a gap of about 2.1-2.2 kW between the supplied heat duty of about 7.9 kW ( ˙ Q evap ) and the sum of the removed heat duties of about 5.7-5.8 kW ( ˙ Q W2 + ˙ Q W3 + ˙ Q W5 ). This gap is due to heat losses, which are unavoidable in such comparatively small plants, even when, as for the plant described here, all relevant parts are equipped with thermal insulation. The different contributions to the heat losses of the plant can be estimated by evaluation of heat balances as described by Notz (2010). As mentioned above, the energy balance and thus heat losses were calculated for different control rooms, e.g. the whole absorption/desorption process or single apparatus and pipeline sections. The results for these different heat losses discussed here for the two experiments 1 and 2 are shown in Table 7. If the control room of the whole absorption desorption process including washing section is considered the heat loss is 2361.2 W for experiment 1 and 2273.5 W for experiment 2. The summation of the heat loss calculation for the seven single control rooms given in Table 7 (from ˙ Q abs + W5 loss to ˙ Q des loss ) yield a heat loss of 2038.7 W for experiment 1 and 1793.9 W for experiment 2. The deviation of approximately 4-6% in relation to the evaporator heat duty gives an idea about the accuracy of calculating the total heat loss of the plant. The reason for this discrepancy can be found in the fact of error propagation for the experimental data, in the calculation of the heat balances for the eight control rooms as well as in model simplifications (e.g. assumption of ideal gas, simplification for the specific heat capacity of the liquid mixture of MEA + H2O + CO2; more details see in Notz, 2010). The value for the heat loss in the absorber including its washing section is only in the order of 100 W due to the low temperature and the insulation of the apparatus. The heat loss in the unisolated but comparatively cold piping from the absorber bottom to the rich-lean heat exchanger W4 is of the order of 200 W, whereas for the hot piping from the rich-lean heat exchanger W4 to the desorber it is of the order of 500 W despite the insulation. The heat loss in the comparatively small heat exchanger W3 for cooling down the lean solvent before entering the absorber is neglected. In the non isolated piping from the heat exchanger W3 to the absorber inlet, a heat loss of about 400 W occurs, which is welcome as it saves cooling water in W3 and thus has no influence on the required evaporator energy. The heat losses discussed so far show similar values for the experiments 1 and 2. This is not the case for the heat losses in the rich-lean heat exchanger W4 and the desorber for which the calculation yields strongly differing numbers for both experiments (cf. Table 7). The differences in the calculated numbers result from the fact that in experiment 2 flashing occurred in the rich-lean heat exchanger W4 whereas this was not the case for experiment 1 (see also Section 4.2). Flashing of CO2 is not taken into account in the heat balances neither for the rich-lean heat exchanger nor for the desorber. Thus desorption enthalpy of CO2, which is required for flashing of CO2 in the rich-lean heat exchanger, is considered as heat loss in the energy balance. On the other hand less CO2 is released in the desorber in that case, but the energy balance is calculated with the assumption, that all CO2 is released in the desorber. As consequence the calculated heat loss of the desorber is too low and the calculated heat loss of the rich-lean heat exchanger is too high. For more details see Notz (2010).

texts.orig: In the following, the procedure for calculating the specific regeneration energy from the pilot plant data by taking into account relevant heat losses is discussed. Not all heat losses shown in Table 7 have an influence on the evaporator energy. Only those have to be accounted for which avoiding would lead to a decrease of the evaporator energy. These are: the heat losses in the lines from the absorber bottom to the desorber top ( ˙ Q pipe 12 loss + ˙ Q pipe 18 loss ), the heat loss in the rich-lean heat exchanger W4 ( ˙ Q W4 loss ) as well as the heat loss in the desorber ( ˙ Q des loss ). Summing up these numbers yields the overall correction that has to be applied ( ˙ Q ∗ loss ). It is given in the last line of Table 7 for the experiments 1 (1476 W) and 2 (1186 W). The results differ by about 300 W, which is 4% of the evaporator energy. This number is in the range of the estimated overall uncertainty of the determination of the evaporator power and the accuracy of the calculation of heat losses respectively. Based on this result the reboiler duty after correction of heat losses is calculated by ˙ Q evap ∗ = ˙ Q evap -˙ Q ∗ loss .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As already mentioned the energy requirement for solvent regeneration can be split up into four contributions. Energy has to be supplied for covering the desorption enthalpy of CO2, generating additional stripping steam, heating up the solvent, and heating up the condensate reflux. The estimation of these four contributions is based on the evaluation of the different terms in the desorber energy balance as explained in detail by Notz (2010). The results are shown in Fig. 13 for the experiments 1 and 2. The main contribution is the energy needed to supply the desorption enthalpy of CO2. The resulting numbers are similar for both experiments (1: 2.35 GJ/tCO 2 , 2: 2.27 GJ/tCO 2 ) which is in line with the observation that the heat of absorption for an aqueous solution of 0.3 g/g MEA is almost independent of the CO2 loading up to a value of approximately ˛ CO2 = 0 . 5 molCO2 / molMEA. The small difference results from the temperature effect of the heat of absorption (Kim and Svendsen, 2007). Thus the specific energy requirement for the desorption of CO2 is representative to the value at large scale plants, if the desorber pressure is the same. Energy is furthermore needed to supply additional stripping steam. As the stripping task is more difficult in experiment 1 than it is in experiment 2, the corresponding number differs (1: 1.76 GJ/tCO 2 , 2: 0.94 GJ/tCO 2 ). The remaining two smaller contributions are similar for both experiments. texts.text: As already mentioned the energy requirement for solvent regeneration can be split up into four contributions. Energy has to be supplied for covering the desorption enthalpy of CO2, generating additional stripping steam, heating up the solvent, and heating up the condensate reflux. The estimation of these four contributions is based on the evaluation of the different terms in the desorber energy balance as explained in detail by Notz (2010). The results are shown in Fig. 13 for the experiments 1 and 2. The main contribution is the energy needed to supply the desorption enthalpy of CO2. The resulting numbers are similar for both experiments (1: 2.35 GJ/tCO 2 , 2: 2.27 GJ/tCO 2 ) which is in line with the observation that the heat of absorption for an aqueous solution of 0.3 g/g MEA is almost independent of the CO2 loading up to a value of approximately ˛ CO2 = 0 . 5 molCO2 / molMEA. The small difference results from the temperature effect of the heat of absorption (Kim and Svendsen, 2007). Thus the specific energy requirement for the desorption of CO2 is representative to the value at large scale plants, if the desorber pressure is the same. Energy is furthermore needed to supply additional stripping steam. As the stripping task is more difficult in experiment 1 than it is in experiment 2, the corresponding number differs (1: 1.76 GJ/tCO 2 , 2: 0.94 GJ/tCO 2 ). The remaining two smaller contributions are similar for both experiments. texts.orig: An overview of the process parameter variation studies that are discussed in the present paper are given in Table 8. The starting point for most of the parameter studies are the two reference experiments 1 and 2, which mainly differ in the CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas (cf. Section 4). In the studies A-D the most important process parameters affecting the capture process are varied as solvent flow rate, desorber pressure, solvent composition and solvent temperature. In the studies E-H boundary conditions are varied as the CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas, the fluid dynamic load of the absorber respectively the F-factor, the CO2 removal rate as well as the flue gas temperature.

texts.orig: An accurate evaluation of the energy requirement in the desorber is essential to draw proper conclusions for the experimental results. Due to the high surface to volume ratio of the small pilot plant, heat losses cannot be neglected. Thus the reboiler duty measured in the experiments must be corrected by relevant heat losses. These were determined here based on energy balances as explained in Section 4.3. The relevant heat losses for all MEA experiments in the pilot plant are approximately 1.2-1.6 kW. This heat loss is calculated and corrected individually for each experiment. In the following, the specific regeneration energy requirement q evap is always given as corrected value. The variable ˙ Q evap ∗ describes the reboiler duty after correction by relevant heat losses, whereas the variable ˙ Q evap denominates the measured electric power.

texts.orig: The detailed analysis of the energy balance for the desorber explained in Section 4.3 shows that the regeneration energy consists of four different contributions, namely: the energy needed for (1) desorption of CO2 (desorption enthalpy), (2) generating stripping stream, (3) heating up of the solvent, and (4) heating up of the condensate reflux. Diagrams with these four contributions to the specific regeneration energy will be shown for some of the parameter studies in the present paper, all diagrams are shown in Notz, 2010. In the following the experimental data are presented and discussed using graphical representations in figures. A full set of the numerical experimental data determined in the present studies is reported as supplementary material and also can be seen in Notz (2010).

This will be discussed below based on the experimental results.

texts.orig: First the absorber is considered. For a known flue gas stream and the specified CO2 removal rate the absorbed CO2 mass flow from the flue gas into the liquid solvent is also fixed. For the variation of the solvent flow rate this means, that the difference between the CO2 loading in rich and lean solvent must vary due to the CO2 mass balance. With an increasing solvent flow rate this difference has to decrease, which can be seen exemplary for the experimental studies A.3 and A.4 in Fig. 14. It has to be noticed that the CO2 mass balance only determines the difference between rich and lean loading. The absolute numbers for the CO2 loadings are determined by the required driving force for the CO2 mass transfer from the gas phase into the liquid phase, which is influenced by mass transfer coefficients, by the reaction kinetics of CO2 to form bicarbonate and carbamate (see for example Freguia and Rochelle, 2003; Blauwhoff et al., 1984) and by the interfacial area respectively the packing height in the absorber. texts.orig: It can be seen in Fig. 15 that the equilibrium curve shows a decrease of the equilibrium loading from the absorber bottom (high CO2 partial pressure) towards the upper section in the absorber to a minimum value. From that point to the top of the absorber (low CO2 partial pressure) the equilibrium loading is increasing again. This behaviour is related to the temperature bulge in the absorber column and to the fact that the equilibrium loading of CO2 in aqueous amine solutions is decreasing with increasing temperature (see for example Böttinger et al. (2008)). Furthermore differences in the equilibrium curves between the different experiments are also related to fluctuations in the MEA content of the solvent between 26.5 wt% and 31.6 wt%. The operating lines in Fig. 15 show an increase of the slope with increasing solvent flow rate. This agrees to the fact, that the operating line is nothing else than a CO2 material balance. In all the experiments the operating line approaches the equilibrium curve in the absorber bottom (high CO2 partial pressure) whereas a significant distance to equilibrium can be observed at the absorber top (low CO2 partial pressure). This is a typical behaviour for the CO2 capture from flue gas by reactive absorption.

texts.orig: heating up of the solvent, and (4) heating up of the reflux condensate. These contributions are shown in Fig. 16. The desorption enthalpy and the stripping steam demand are the main contributions to the regeneration energy requirement followed by the energy to heat up the solvent flow. The energy to heat up the condensate reflux usually is almost negligible. The results in Fig. 16a and b show a much higher stripping steam requirement for the experiments A.3 compared to the experiments A.4 which corresponds to the lower lean loading for the experiments A.3 shown in Fig. 14. This is due to the lower CO2 partial pressure in the experiments A.3. Furthermore the experiments show a drastic increase of the stripping steam for low solvent flow rates, which is related to the low required lean loading (cf. Fig. 14). The desorption enthalpy only slightly changes by variation of the solvent flow rate because of a temperature change in the desorber top. The influence of the temperature on the desorption enthalpy is high whereas the influence of the CO2 loading is low in the relevant loading range, which is shown by Kim and Svendsen (2007). The energy contribution to heat the solvent increases almost linearly with increasing solvent flow rate. This corresponds to the expectation if the assumption is made that the heat capacity and the temperature difference of the rich-lean heat exchanger are constant. The total specific energy requirement is determined by all four contributions mentioned above. Since the stripping steam is increasing for a lower solvent flow rate and the energy requirement to heat the solvent is increasing with an increasing solvent flow rate a minimum specific energy requirement is expected at a certain optimum solvent flow rate. This behaviour for the total specific energy requirement for the variation studies A.1 to A.4 is shown in Fig. 17. For the experiments A.1-A.3 with a CO2 partial pressure of approximately 54-57 mbar the optimum solvent flow rate is found in the considered solvent flow range whereas the optimum solvent flow rate for the experiments A.4 is less than 150 kg/h.

texts.orig: The temperature level in the desorber and in the evaporator is directly connected with the desorber pressure through the boiling conditions of the solvent, which are dominated by that one of water. Increasing the desorber pressure leads to a rise in the desorber temperature as can be seen in Fig. 18a for the experimental series B. As consequence the equilibrium isotherms of the CO2 solubility shift to lower loadings at constant CO2 partial pressure which means that the regeneration task is getting easier. Since all operation parameters beside the desorber pressure are kept constant in these experiments (cf. Table 10), the regeneration is more effective with increasing desorber pressure. This leads to a decrease of the lean loading, which is shown in Fig. 18b. Furthermore the difference between rich and lean loading is increasing by approximately 11% when the desorber pressure is increased from 1 bar to 2.3 bar. This means, that the absorbed CO2 mass flow rate is increased as well. As consequence the specific energy requirement for solvent regeneration decreases with a higher desorber pressure as can be seen in Fig. 19a. As explained above the reason for that behaviour can be found in a shift of the equilibrium isotherms for the CO2 solubility. This means that less stripping steam is required for the separation task, what can be seen in Fig. 19b. Furthermore Fig. 19b shows a slight increase of the desorption enthalpy by the rising desorber pressure which is related to the temperature dependence of the heat of absorption. The specific energy requirement to heat up the solvent flow rate is increasing with an increase in the desorber pressure, which is related to the increasing temperature difference in the rich-lean heat exchanger. This effect could be reduced by using a rich-lean heat exchanger with a larger surface area.

The distance between equilibrium isotherms at high temperature (desorber conditions) and low temperatures (absorber conditions), namely the cyclic capacity, primarily influences the solvent flow rate. Coming back to the four contributions to the regeneration energy, it can be said, that the solvent composition directly influences the required energy to heat up the solvent flow.

The effect of the MEA mass fraction on the equilibrium isotherm at 50 ◦ C is shown in Fig. 20a. The increase of the MEA mass fraction from approximately 10 wt% via 22.5 wt% to 30 wt% leads to a higher solubility of CO2. This behaviour can be explained more in detail with Fig. 23b, in which the absorber operating line is plotted versus the calculated equilibrium curve for 30 wt% MEA at 50 ◦ C. In that diagram the horizontal dotted line represents an estimation of the distance between the operating line and the equilibrium isotherm for the CO2 partial pressure at the absorber bottom for each single experiment of that experimental study. It can be seen that for the conditions at the absorber bottom the rich loading is reaching more than 90% of the equilibrium value for the experiments with a higher CO2 partial pressure (85.1 mbar, 109.4 mbar, 133.7 mbar) whereas the distance to the equilibrium value is much higher for the experiments with a low CO2 partial pressure (35.5 mbar, 54.6 mbar). This means, that a high driving force for the CO2 mass transfer in the absorber is required for the pilot plant operation with a low CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas due to low overall absorption kinetics and a limited absorber packing height of 4.2 m. A better approach to equilibrium could only be reached by increasing the interfacial area respectively the packing height in the absorber.

texts.orig: As result from the discussion above in this section about the relation between the position of the absorber operating line and the equilibrium curve (cf. Fig. 23b) the following conclusion can be drawn. The minimum achievable energy requirement only can be reached, if the absorber operating line equals the absorber equilibrium curve at one point, typically in the absorber bottom for the application of CO2 absorption from flue gas. To ensure a minimum integral driving force for the CO2 mass transfer, a sufficient interfacial area has to be offered. In the variation study considered here, the distance to equilibrium could be reduced for the experiments with low CO2 partial pressure by a larger interfacial area between gas and liquid phase. This would result in a larger lean loading and lower specific regeneration energy in the desorber (see also discussion in Section 4).

texts.orig: The goal of this parameter study is to investigate the effect of reducing both the flue gas flow rate and the solvent flow rate at a constant L / G ratio with otherwise unchanged conditions and specifications. This reflects the need in flexibility of the operation of a large scale plant with regard to load change of the power plant. Furthermore these experiments show a method for solvent comparison, if the packing height in the absorber is not sufficient due to low overall absorption kinetics.

texts.orig: The conclusion of these results for the design of a large scale plant is that the absorber needs to be designed for the full load case or the maximum flue gas flow rate respectively. Especially the absorber packing height needs to be designed that way, that the rich loading approaches the equilibrium curve as much as possible for the full load case. Thus also for part load operation the absorber operating line will approach the equilibrium curve as much as possible. For the determination of the turn down ratio with regard to the flue gas flow rate and the solvent flow rate it has to be kept in mind that the wetting of the packing cannot be maintained below a certain fluid dynamic load and thus the interfacial area for the mass transfer will go down. Thus the lower limit for the flue gas flow rate is determined by the fluid dynamic limitation of the selected packing.

texts.orig: The absorber temperature profile and especially the temperature at the absorber bottom have two contrarious effects which could influence the regeneration energy requirement. On the one hand a lower temperature leads to a higher equilibrium loading for CO2, which could result in an increase in the rich and lean loading. Thus the regeneration energy requirement could be reduced. On the other hand reaction kinetics and thus the absorption kinetics are reduced by a temperature decrease. This might lead to a reduction in the approach of the rich solvent to the equilibrium loading at the absorber bottom, if the absorber packing height is not sufficient. As consequence a reduction in the rich loading would lead to a reduced lean loading, if all other operation parameters are kept constant, and thus would lead to an increased regeneration energy requirement.

No significant temperature effect can be observed. The reason for that might be found in the two contrarious effects of the temperature influence on the equilibrium solubility as well as on the absorption kinetics as discussed above. For the pilot plant the absorber packing height is too short. This means, that even if the equilibrium loading at the absorber bottom is increased by a lower temperature, the rich loading does not increase due to the lower absorption kinetics. Because of that the conclusion of this study cannot be comprehensive for a large scale plant with a sufficient absorber height.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Conclusion
texts.text: 6. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main task in optimizing the process for CO2 removal from power plant flue gases by reactive absorption is the reduction of the energy requirement. This can be achieved by optimizing equipment and operation, and using new improved solvents. As modelling and simulation of the complex physico-chemical processes in the absorption/desorption plant is difficult for new solvents, experimental investigations are needed. The paper presents a pilot plant which is suited for such studies. The operation and the evaluation of the results are explained in detail using two experiments with MEA as reference case. It is shown how, only based on mass and energy balances, a wealth of consistent information can be obtained from the experiments that gives deep insight in the process and the options for optimization. Based on the pilot plant experiments it is shown, that the energy requirement for solvent regeneration can be divided into four contributions, which are named in the following in the order of descending importance: energy has to be supplied for covering the desorption enthalpy of CO2, generating additional stripping steam, heating up the solvent, and heating up the condensate reflux.
texts.text: The main task in optimizing the process for CO2 removal from power plant flue gases by reactive absorption is the reduction of the energy requirement. This can be achieved by optimizing equipment and operation, and using new improved solvents. As modelling and simulation of the complex physico-chemical processes in the absorption/desorption plant is difficult for new solvents, experimental investigations are needed. The paper presents a pilot plant which is suited for such studies. The operation and the evaluation of the results are explained in detail using two experiments with MEA as reference case. It is shown how, only based on mass and energy balances, a wealth of consistent information can be obtained from the experiments that gives deep insight in the process and the options for optimization. Based on the pilot plant experiments it is shown, that the energy requirement for solvent regeneration can be divided into four contributions, which are named in the following in the order of descending importance: energy has to be supplied for covering the desorption enthalpy of CO2, generating additional stripping steam, heating up the solvent, and heating up the condensate reflux.

texts.orig: Furthermore the present paper reports on results of systematic studies of the CO2 capture with MEA that were carried out in the pilot plant. The influence of the operation parameters solvent flow rate, desorber pressure, solvent composition and solvent temperature on the regeneration energy requirement is investigated. Furthermore the influence of the boundary conditions CO2 partial pressure, fluid dynamic load, CO2 removal rate and flue gas temperature on the regeneration energy requirement is analysed. On the one hand the results provide a comprehensive basis of experimental data for the evaluation of the potential for a reduction of the regeneration energy by using new solvents as well as for validation of simulation tools. On the other hand the results allow gaining a comprehensive understanding of the absorption/desorption process for CO2 capture from flue gas, which leads to the following conclusions.

An increasing temperature leads to a shift of the equilibrium isotherm of the CO2 solubility towards lower loadings at a constant CO2 partial pressure. Thus the separation task in the desorber is getting easier, which leads to a reduction in the stripping steam demand and a decrease in the regeneration energy requirement. For selection of the desorber pressure respectively temperature it has to be kept in mind, that an upper limit might be determined by an inacceptable increase in solvent degradation.

The variation of the solvent concentration mainly influences the equilibrium isotherms, which again determine the cyclic capacity. The heat of absorption especially can be influenced by variation of the type of the solvent.

texts.orig: The variation of the absorber inlet temperature of the flue gas and the solvent influences both the reaction rates in the liquid phase as well as the equilibrium solubility of CO2. The impact of these two physico-chemical properties is opposite: a decrease of the temperature leads to an increase in the CO2 solubility under absorber conditions which is favourable for the regeneration energy requirement. On the other hand a temperature decrease leads to reduced absorption kinetics, which leads to an increase in the regeneration energy requirement if the absorber packing height is not sufficient. Furthermore it is important to keep in mind that the extent of the temperature effect depends on the L / G ratio. For the temperature variation it has to be taken into account that the overall water balance of the absorption/desorption process has to be fulfilled.
texts.text: The variation of the absorber inlet temperature of the flue gas and the solvent influences both the reaction rates in the liquid phase as well as the equilibrium solubility of CO2. The impact of these two physico-chemical properties is opposite: a decrease of the temperature leads to an increase in the CO2 solubility under absorber conditions which is favourable for the regeneration energy requirement. On the other hand a temperature decrease leads to reduced absorption kinetics, which leads to an increase in the regeneration energy requirement if the absorber packing height is not sufficient. Furthermore it is important to keep in mind that the extent of the temperature effect depends on the L / G ratio. For the temperature variation it has to be taken into account that the overall water balance of the absorption/desorption process has to be fulfilled.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The variation of the fluid dynamic load and of the CO2 removal rate especially illustrates the influence of the required driving force for the CO2 mass transfer in the absorber on the CO2 loading in the rich and lean solvent and thus on the energy requirement. The results make clear, that a sufficient absorber packing height is important for the optimization of the regeneration energy requirement. Thus the absorber height should be designed for the maximum required flue gas flow rate and the maximum required CO2 removal rate. For the determination of the turn down ratio with regard to the flue gas flow rate and thus the solvent flow rate it has to be kept in mind that the wetting of the packing cannot be maintained below a certain fluid dynamic load and thus the interfacial area for the mass transfer will go down.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Appendix A. Evaluation of column profiles
texts.text: Appendix A. Evaluation of column profiles

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The calculation of column profiles for not measured data in absorber and desorber like partial pressure profiles for CO2 and H2O and mass transfer profiles for CO2 and H2O is based on the measurement of column profiles for the temperature and the CO2 mass fraction in the liquid phase. In addition the flow rate and the composition of gas and liquid stream at the bottom of each column is required as input. Furthermore it is assumed, that only CO2 and H2O show a significant mass transfer. This is justified since N2 and O2 show a low solubility in the liquid phase and MEA has a low volatility compared to H2O. It has to be noticed, that this assumption is suitable to calculate the column profiles for CO2 and H2O, nevertheless it can not be applied for the evaluation of amine loss by volatility and the calculation of the O2 content in the liquid phase with regard to oxidative degradation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the calculation of column profiles in detail the following procedure is used:
texts.text: For the calculation of column profiles in detail the following procedure is used:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · In the absorber, the component mass balances and the energy balance are solved for each packing segment starting at the absorber bottom. Thereby the assumption of an adiabatic column is taken. In addition, for the calculation of the condensation of water vapour in the absorber washing section, only H2O mass transfer is assumed.
texts.text: In the absorber, the component mass balances and the energy balance are solved for each packing segment starting at the absorber bottom. Thereby the assumption of an adiabatic column is taken. In addition, for the calculation of the condensation of water vapour in the absorber washing section, only H2O mass transfer is assumed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · For the evaluation of the desorber profile, first the evaporation in the desorber bottom is calculated as equilibrium stage. In addition to component mass balances and the energy balance, the equilibrium condition is considered. Thus the CO2 partial pressure in the gas phase is taken as the corresponding equilibrium value to the known lean loading. Based on the results, the component mass balances and the energy balance are solved for each packing segment. For the calculation of the desorber washing section, the component material balance for H2O is applied together with the assumption of water saturation in the gas stream.

In addition to component mass balances and the energy balance, the equilibrium condition is considered. Thus the CO2 partial pressure in the gas phase is taken as the corresponding equilibrium value to the known lean loading. Based on the results, the component mass balances and the energy balance are solved for each packing segment. For the calculation of the desorber washing section, the component material balance for H2O is applied together with the assumption of water saturation in the gas stream.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The CO2 mass transfer in the flash vessel at the desorber inlet of the rich solvent is calculated with component material balance based on the measured CO2 content in the solvent at the sampling points V80 and V19.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B.1. Determination of the amine content by gas chromatography
texts.text: B.1. Determination of the amine content by gas chromatography

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the analysis of the amine content in liquid samples the gas chromatograph HP6890 (Agilent) equipped with the capillary column RTX5 Amine (30 m × 0.25 mm × 1.0 mm (Restek)) and a flame ionisation detector (FID) is applied. Helium is used as carrier gas. For the quantitative analysis of the amine mass fraction, the GC method is calibrated with the internal standard 1-propanol (Carl Roth, 1-Propanol ROTIPURAN ® ≥ 99.5% p.a., Art.-Nr. 6776). For the determination of the amine mass fraction of a liquid sample, three GC runs are carried out to judge the reproducibility and to reduce the measurement error by averaging.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B.3. Determination of the CO2 content by titration with KOH
texts.text: B.3. Determination of the CO2 content by titration with KOH

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 mass fraction in liquid samples is determined by titration with a KOH solution in ethanol (Merck, KOH in ethanol, c(KOH) = 0.5 mol/l, Art.-Nr. 109114). For that purpose a Basic Titrino 794 (Metrohm) is used. For titration the liquid sample is given into a mixture of ethanol + water (water from own bidistillation; ethanol denaturated by 1% ethyl methyl ketone p.a.; Merck, Art.Nr. 100974). For the determination of the CO2 mass fraction in a liquid sample, the analysis is carried out at least twice to judge the reproducibility and to reduce the measurement error by averaging.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B.4. Determination of the CO2 content by the BaCl2 method
texts.text: B.4. Determination of the CO2 content by the BaCl2 method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to the CO2 analysis by KOH titration as explained above, the more complex BaCl2 method is applied for various samples for comparison reasons. In a first step of this method the carbamate ions and bicarbonate ions in the liquid sample are precipitated as barium carbonate by adding of BaCl2 (Merck, barium chloride dihydrate, Art.-Nr. 101719). In a second step, the solid BaCO3 has to be filtrated and quantitatively transferred into a beaker. Then the solid is dissolved with an excess of hydrochloric acid (Merck, hydrochloric acid c (HCl) = 0.5 mol/l, Art.-Nr. 109058). The amount of unreacted hydrochloric acid is determined by titration with NaOH (Merck, sodium hydoxide c (KOH) = 0.5 mol/l, Art.-Nr. 109138). Finally the CO2 mass fraction can be calculated from the difference of the initially applied amount of HCl and the unreacted amount of HCl. The BaCl2 method is more time consuming and error-prone due to the numerous steps of the analytical procedure. Especially it has to be taken into account, that the solid BaCO3 is completely captured by filtration and transferred into the beaker. Thus the reproducibility of that method is approximately 3-4%, which leads to a little bit higher scattering in the results compared to the KOH titration (reproducibility approx. 1-2%). For the determination of the CO2 mass fraction in a liquid sample, the analysis is carried out at least twice to judge the reproducibility and to reduce the measurement error by averaging. The comparison of the calculated absorbed CO2 mass flow in the absorber based on the BaCl method on the one hand and based on the KOH titration on the other hand shows similar results. The KOH titration is recommended as standard method.
